title: A History of China
description: A History of China — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "A History of China" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.description: "Transcriber's note and front matter for A History of China"

memes.label: Table of Contents
memes.description: Chapter-by-chapter table of contents for A History of China

memes.label: An Lu-shan
memes.description: A T'ang dynasty military governor of Sogdian-Turkic origin whose rebellion from 755 to 763 CE devastated the empire and marked the end of its golden age.

memes.label: An Lu-shan Rebellion
memes.description: The An Lu-shan Rebellion (755–763 CE) was a devastating military uprising led by the Sogdian-Turkish general An Lu-shan against the T'ang dynasty, marking the beginning of the dynasty's irreversible decline and the transition from medieval to modern China.

memes.label: Boxer Rising
memes.description: The Boxer Rising (1900) was an anti-foreign popular movement in late Ch'ing China, initially directed against the Manchu government and gentry, which the dowager empress Tz'u Hsi redirected against Europeans, provoking international military intervention and the occupation of Peking.

memes.label: Buddhism in China
memes.description: Buddhism is a major world religion that entered China from India via Central Asia during the Han dynasty and profoundly transformed Chinese culture, philosophy, art, and social life over two millennia.

memes.label: Chang Ch'ien
memes.description: A Han dynasty diplomat and explorer whose journey to the western regions in the 2nd century BCE opened Chinese trade and diplomatic relations with Central Asia, laying the foundation for the Silk Road.

memes.label: Chang Hsien-chung
memes.description: Late Ming rebel leader (1606–1647) who, alongside Li Tzu-ch'eng, led one of the major peasant insurrections that contributed to the fall of the Ming dynasty and the Manchu conquest of China.

memes.label: Chang Hsüeh-liang
memes.description: Chang Hsüeh-liang (1901–2001), the "Young Marshal" of Manchuria, known for his role in the Sian Incident of 1936 when he kidnapped Chiang Kai-shek to force a united Chinese front against Japanese invasion.

memes.label: Chang Ling
memes.description: Founder of the first organised Taoist church in the second century A.D., whose theocratic community in Szechwan introduced a lasting sacerdotal dimension to Chinese religion and whose movement foreshadowed the great Taoist rebellions of late Han.

memes.label: Chang Tsai
memes.description: A Northern Sung Neo-Confucian philosopher who, with Chou Tun-i, developed a cosmological theory that challenged Buddhist metaphysics and articulated the concept of ch'i as the fundamental substance of the universe.

memes.label: Ch'en Tu-hsiu
memes.description: Founding editor of the journal "New Youth" (1915) and a central leader of the May Fourth Movement, who became one of the first leaders of the Chinese Communist Party from 1920.

memes.label: Ch'eng Hao
memes.description: Neo-Confucian philosopher of the Sung dynasty (1032–1085) who, together with his brother Ch'eng I, developed a metaphysical account of human nature based on the concept of Material Force (ch'i), laying foundations for the philosophical system later perfected by Chu Hsi.

memes.label: Cheng Ho
memes.description: Ming dynasty eunuch admiral who led seven naval expeditions across the Indian Ocean between 1405 and 1433, reaching Arabia and establishing Chinese maritime prestige throughout southern Asia.

memes.label: Ch'eng I
memes.description: A leading Neo-Confucian philosopher of the Northern Sung who, with his brother Ch'eng Hao, developed the concept of li (principle) as the foundation of Neo-Confucian metaphysics.

memes.label: Chiang Kai-shek
memes.description: Chinese military and political leader (1887–1975) who headed the Kuomintang (Nationalist) government of China from 1928 to 1949 and later presided over the Republic of China in Taiwan.

memes.label: Ch'ien-lung
memes.description: Emperor of the Manchu Ch'ing dynasty (r. 1736–1796) whose reign marked both the greatest territorial expansion and the first signs of internal decline of the Manchu empire.

memes.label: Chin Dynasty
memes.description: Imperial dynasty of China (A.D. 265–419) divided into the Western Chin (265–317) and Eastern Chin (317–419) periods, which briefly reunified China after the Three Kingdoms and later ruled the south during the era of division.

memes.label: Chinese Art
memes.description: Chinese art encompasses painting, sculpture, ceramics, bronze-casting, lacquerware, and other visual arts developed over more than four millennia of Chinese civilization, shaped by indigenous traditions, Buddhist influence, and periodic contact with foreign cultures.

memes.label: Ch'ing Dynasty
memes.description: The last imperial dynasty of China (1644-1911), founded by the Manchu people, which presided over a period of territorial expansion, cultural achievement, and ultimately revolutionary transformation.

memes.label: Chou Dynasty
memes.description: The longest-ruling dynasty in Chinese history (c. 1028–256 B.C.), founded by Wu Wang through the conquest of the Shang, divided into the Western Chou (c. 1028–771 B.C.) and Eastern Chou (770–256 B.C.) periods, which established the feudal, cultural, and philosophical foundations of Chinese civilization.

memes.label: Chou En-lai
memes.description: Chinese Communist revolutionary and statesman who served as the first Premier of the People's Republic of China (1949–1976), a key figure in the Chinese Communist Party from its founding.

memes.label: Chou Tun-i
memes.description: A seminal Neo-Confucian philosopher of the Northern Sung who, together with Chang Tsai, developed a cosmological theory rivaling Buddhist metaphysics.

memes.label: Chu Ch'üan-chung
memes.description: Chu Ch'üan-chung (852–912), founder and first emperor of the Later Liang dynasty (907–923), a former Tang military commander who ended the Tang dynasty and ushered in the Five Dynasties period.

memes.label: Chu Hsi
memes.description: Chinese Neo-Confucian philosopher (1130–1200) of the Sung dynasty whose synthesis of Confucian thought became the orthodox state ideology of China until the early twentieth century.

memes.label: Chu-ko Liang
memes.description: Premier, regent, and chief strategist of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms period, whose far-seeing political plans sustained the state and whose death in 234 marked the beginning of Shu Han's decline.

memes.label: Chu Tê
memes.description: Founding father of the Chinese Red Army (1886–1976), commander-in-chief of Communist forces during the Civil War and Sino-Japanese War.

memes.label: Chu Yüan-chang
memes.description: Founder and first emperor of the Ming dynasty (r. 1368–1398), who rose from peasant origins to unify China after the Mongol Yuan period.

memes.label: Chuang Tzu
memes.description: Chinese Taoist philosopher (4th century BC), regarded as the most gifted poet among Chinese philosophers, whose eponymous work developed Lao Tzu's teachings with unparalleled literary brilliance and imaginative parables.

memes.label: Confucianism
memes.description: Confucianism is a Chinese ethical and philosophical system developed from the teachings of Confucius that became the dominant state ideology of imperial China, shaping its political institutions, educational system, and social values for over two thousand years.

memes.label: Confucius
memes.description: Chinese philosopher (c. 551–479 BCE) of the Spring and Autumn period whose ethical and political teachings became the foundation of Confucianism, the dominant state ideology of imperial China for over two millennia.

memes.label: Dalai Lama
memes.description: The Dalai Lama, the supreme religious ruler of Tibet and the highest spiritual authority in Tibetan Buddhism, recognised as the incarnation of Avalokiteshvara.

memes.label: Wolfram Eberhard
memes.description: Wolfram Eberhard (1909–1989), German-born American sinologist and author of "A History of China," a widely-used English-language survey of Chinese history first published in 1950.

memes.label: Eunuchs
memes.description: Eunuchs were castrated servants of the imperial palace who, by virtue of their access to the emperor and their control over the inner court, repeatedly emerged as a powerful political faction in Chinese history, shaping the course of dynastic politics from the Han dynasty through the Ming dynasty.

memes.label: Imperial Examination System
memes.description: The imperial examination system (k'o-chu) was the primary method of selecting civil officials in imperial China from c. 100 BCE until 1904 CE, based on mastery of the Confucian classics rather than birth or military merit.

memes.label: Fan Chung-yen
memes.description: A Sung dynasty statesman, reformer, and literary figure who created the clan estate (i-chuang) system, founding an enduring institution of Chinese social organization.

memes.label: Fêng Yü-hsiang
memes.description: A prominent Republican-era Chinese warlord known as the "Christian General," who controlled significant territory in northern China and alternated between alliance and conflict with Chiang Kai-shek.

memes.label: Fu Chien
memes.description: Fu Chien (r. 357–385), a Tibetan ruler of the Former Qin state who unified all of north China through military innovation and sought to become emperor of a culturally Chinese realm, only to be destroyed by his failed southern campaign of 383.

memes.label: Galdan
memes.description: A Mongol prince and Dzungar leader who attempted to establish an independent Mongol realm free from Manchu-Chinese influence in the late seventeenth century, provoking the Kang-hsi Emperor's personal military campaigns (1690–1696).

memes.label: Gentry
memes.description: The gentry (士绅/士族) was the dominant social class of imperial China, emerging during the Han dynasty as a landowning, educated elite whose families combined agricultural estate management with official positions in the state bureaucracy, and who maintained their status through Confucian education, strategic marriages, and control of the examination system for over two thousand years.

memes.label: Han Dynasty
memes.description: Imperial dynasty of China (206 B.C.–A.D. 220) founded by Liu Pang (Kao Tsu), which consolidated the imperial system, established Confucianism as state ideology, and established the gentry state that endured for two millennia.

memes.label: Han Fei Tzu
memes.description: The most prominent Legalist philosopher of ancient China (died 233 BCE), who synthesized earlier Legalist ideas into a coherent theory of statecraft.

memes.label: Han Yü
memes.description: Han Yü (768–825), the foremost essayist of the T'ang dynasty and a leading figure in the Classical Prose Movement, known for his vigorous defense of Confucianism against Buddhist and Taoist influences.

memes.label: Hsiao Tao-ch'eng
memes.description: A general of the Liu Sung dynasty who murdered the imperial family and founded the Southern Ch'i dynasty (479–501), continuing the pattern of military usurpation that characterized the Southern Dynasties period.

memes.label: Hsiung-nu
memes.description: The Hsiung-nu (匈奴) were a nomadic tribal confederation on the northern frontier of China who established the first great steppe empire, challenged the Han dynasty as an equal power, and whose descendants founded Hun kingdoms in North China during the period of division.

memes.label: Hsüan-tsang
memes.description: Renowned Chinese Buddhist monk and pilgrim of the T'ang dynasty who travelled to India and brought back Buddhist scriptures for translation.

memes.label: Hsüan Tsung
memes.description: T'ang emperor (r. 712–756) whose long reign marked both the cultural zenith and the catastrophic turning-point of the dynasty, ending with the An Lu-shan Rebellion and his forced abdication.

memes.label: Hsün Tzu
memes.description: Confucian philosopher of the Warring States period (c. 298–238 BC) who argued that human nature is inherently bad but can be improved through education and ritual.

memes.label: Hu Shih
memes.description: Leading Chinese liberal intellectual and pragmatist philosopher of the early twentieth century, a central figure in the New Culture Movement and the Literary Revolution who advocated the use of vernacular Chinese (pai-hua) in writing.

memes.label: Huai-nan Tzu
memes.description: Prince Liu An of the Han dynasty, known as Huai-nan Tzu, an early Taoist philosopher, alchemist, and patron of scholars whose writings represent one of the three most important intellectual works of the Han period.

memes.label: Huan Hsüan
memes.description: Son of the general Huan Wen who briefly usurped the Eastern Chin throne in A.D. 403–404, proclaiming his own "Ch'u" dynasty before being defeated by Liu Yü.

memes.label: Huan Wen
memes.description: Eastern Chin dynasty general (312–373) who dominated the court through military power and attempted to seize the throne.

memes.label: Huang Ch'ao
memes.description: Leader of the massive peasant rebellion (874–884) that effectively ended the T'ang dynasty and transformed Chinese social structure.

memes.label: Huang Tsung-hsi
memes.description: Ming dynasty scholar (1610–1695) who was the first Chinese thinker to question monarchy as the only form of government, writing a treatise on non-monarchic rule in 1662.

memes.label: K'ang-hsi
memes.description: The second emperor of the Manchu Ch'ing dynasty (r. 1662–1722), whose sixty-year reign consolidated Manchu rule over China and initiated a major cultural flourishing.

memes.label: Kao Tsu
memes.description: Founder and first emperor of the Han dynasty (r. 202–195 B.C.), who rose from peasant origins to unify China after the fall of the Ch'in and established a pattern of gentry-supported governance that endured for two millennia.

memes.label: Kao Tsung
memes.description: T'ang dynasty emperor (r. 650–683), son of T'ai Tsung, whose reign saw the completion of the Korean conquest and the rise of Empress Wu.

memes.label: Ku Yen-wu
memes.description: Ming-Qing scholar (1613–1682) and geographer whose encyclopaedic work on historical geography laid the foundations for the "Archaeological School" and evidential research (k'ao-cheng) movement.

memes.label: Kuang-wu Ti (Emperor Guangwu)
memes.description: Kuang-wu Ti (Liu Hsiu, 5 BC–AD 57), founder and first emperor of the Later Han dynasty, restored Han rule after the fall of Wang Mang's usurpation.

memes.label: Kung-sun Lung
memes.description: A leading figure of the School of Names (Dialecticians) during the Warring States period, known for his logical paradoxes and debates on the relationship between names and reality.

memes.label: Kuo Mo-jo
memes.description: Twentieth-century Chinese historian, archaeologist, and writer whose research on oracle bones, bronze inscriptions, and ancient Chinese society shaped modern understanding of early China.

memes.label: Lao Tzu
memes.description: Legendary Chinese philosopher (4th century BC), traditionally regarded as the founder of Taoism and author of the Tao Tê Ching, whose doctrine of wu-wei (non-action) profoundly shaped Chinese religion, philosophy, and political thought.

memes.label: Li Hung-chang
memes.description: A leading Qing dynasty statesman, diplomat, and military modernizer who played a central role in China's Self-Strengthening Movement and late imperial foreign relations.

memes.label: Li Lin-fu
memes.description: Li Lin-fu (d. 752), powerful chancellor of the Tang dynasty under Emperor Hsüan-tsung, known for his capable administration and political manoeuvring that contributed to the An Lu-shan Rebellion.

memes.label: Li Lung-mien
memes.description: Li Lung-mien (Li Gonglin, ca. 1040–1106), renowned Chinese painter of the Northern Sung dynasty, celebrated for his horse paintings and figure compositions in the literati tradition.

memes.label: Li Po
memes.description: Greatest romantic poet of the T'ang dynasty (701–762), known for his lyrical genius and Taoist-influenced verse.

memes.label: Li Yüan
memes.description: The founder and first emperor of the T'ang dynasty, who overthrew the Sui in 618 with the military leadership of his son Li Shih-min.

memes.label: Li Yüan-hung
memes.description: Li Yüan-hung (1864–1928), Chinese military officer and politician who served as vice-president and later president of the Republic of China during the early Republican period.

memes.label: Liang Ch'i-ch'ao
memes.description: A leading Chinese scholar, journalist, and reformist intellectual of the late Qing and early Republican periods who advocated constitutional monarchy and introduced Western political thought to China.

memes.label: Lin Tsê-hsü
memes.description: A Qing dynasty scholar-official best known for his forceful opposition to the opium trade and his role as Imperial Commissioner at Canton during the crisis that led to the First Opium War.

memes.label: Liu Chin
memes.description: The dominant eunuch leader of the early sixteenth-century Ming court, whose briefly supreme power (1505–1510) and subsequent downfall exemplified the recurring pattern of eunuch ascendancy and collapse in late imperial China.

memes.label: Liu Lao-chih
memes.description: A general of the Eastern Jin dynasty who played a pivotal role in the power struggles leading to the rise of Huan Hsüan and later Liu Yü.

memes.label: Liu Pei
memes.description: Liu Pei, a warlord of the late Han dynasty and founder of the Shu Han kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his claim of legitimate succession to the Han imperial line.

memes.label: Liu Shao-ch'i
memes.description: Chinese communist political leader and Chairman of the People's Republic of China, who became Mao Tse-tung's principal rival in the 1966 power struggle and was purged during the Cultural Revolution.

memes.label: Liu Yao
memes.description: Liu Yao (d. AD 329), ruler of the Former Zhao state during the Sixteen Kingdoms period, the last Xiongnu ruler of the Liu Yuan dynasty before its destruction by Shih Lo.

memes.label: Liu Yüan
memes.description: Leader of the Hsiung-nu (Huns) who in A.D. 304 founded the first of the "Sixteen Kingdoms" in northern China, claiming the Chinese imperial surname Liu and styling his regime the "Han dynasty" to legitimise foreign rule.

memes.label: Lorcha War
memes.description: The Lorcha War (1856–1860), also known as the Arrow War or Second Opium War, was a military conflict between Britain (joined by France) and Ch'ing China, triggered by the Chinese seizure of a British-flagged vessel, resulting in the Treaty of Tientsin and the further expansion of European privileges in China.

memes.label: Lu Hsiang-shan
memes.description: A Sung dynasty Neo-Confucian philosopher who developed the doctrine of intuitive knowledge, emphasizing direct inner realization over book learning, and a key precursor to the Wang Yang-ming school.

memes.label: Lu Hsün
memes.description: Foremost Chinese writer of the early 20th century (1881–1936), a leading figure of the May Fourth Movement and founder of modern Chinese literature.

memes.label: Lü Pu-wei
memes.description: Lü Pu-wei (d. 235 BC), a merchant who rose to become Prime Minister and regent of the Qin state, the first commoner in Chinese history to attain such power, and whose dismissal opened the way for the Legalist conquest that unified China.

memes.label: Mao Tse-tung
memes.description: Leader of the Chinese Communist Party (1935–1976) who imposed a European-derived communist ideology on China and presided over the Cultural Revolution.

memes.label: Mao Tun
memes.description: Second ruler of the Hsiung-nu (Xiongnu) empire (r. c. 209–174 BCE) who built the first great steppe confederation, nearly defeated the Han founder Kao Tsu, and established the precedent of nomadic empires confronting China on equal terms.

memes.label: May Fourth Movement
memes.description: The May Fourth Movement (1919) was an intellectual and political awakening of modern China, sparked by student demonstrations in Peking on May 4, 1919, against the government's pro-Japanese policies, which catalyzed a broader cultural revolution embracing Western science, democracy, and literary reform.

memes.label: Mencius
memes.description: Confucian philosopher of the Warring States period (c. 372–289 BC) who articulated the theory of the Mandate of Heaven as expressed through the voice of the people.

memes.label: Ming Dynasty
memes.description: The Ming dynasty (1368–1644 CE) was the last native Chinese imperial dynasty, founded by the peasant-rebel Chu Yuan-chang, known for its early maritime expeditions, agricultural revolution, literary flourishing, and eventual collapse amidst factional struggles and peasant rebellions.

memes.label: Ming Ti
memes.description: Emperor of the Later Han (r. 58–75 CE) during whose reign Buddhism was traditionally said to have first reached China.

memes.label: Mo Ti
memes.description: Founder of the Mohist school of philosophy during the Warring States period, known for advocating universal love and opposition to offensive warfare.

memes.label: Mongols
memes.description: The Mongols were a nomadic people of the eastern Asian steppe who, under Genghiz Khan and his successors, built the largest contiguous land empire in history and ruled all of China as the Yuan dynasty (1280–1368), the first alien people to govern the whole of Chinese territory.

memes.label: Nien Fei Rebellion
memes.description: A peasant rebellion in Honan and Anhui provinces (c. 1855–1868) that occurred contemporaneously with the T'ai P'ing Rebellion and was suppressed by the Qing government.

memes.label: Opium War (First Opium War)
memes.description: The 1839–1842 war between Qing China and Great Britain, triggered by the Chinese prohibition of the opium trade, ending with the Treaty of Nanking and the cession of Hong Kong.

memes.label: Ou-yang Hsiu
memes.description: A distinguished Song dynasty historian, anti-Buddhist conservative, and pioneer of book censorship who opposed the reforms of Wang An-shih.

memes.label: Pan Ch'ao
memes.description: Pan Ch'ao (32–102 CE), Han dynasty general and diplomat who led a thirty-year campaign to bring the Western Regions under Chinese control during the 1st century CE.

memes.label: Peking
memes.description: Peking (modern Beijing) is the historic northern capital of China, serving as the seat of imperial power under the Liao, Chin, Yuan, Ming, Ch'ing, and modern Communist governments. Located in Hopei province near the Mongols' homeland, its strategic position on the North China Plain made it a pivotal centre of political, economic, and cultural life for over a millennium.

memes.label: Po Chü-i
memes.description: One of the most popular and widely read poets of the T'ang dynasty (772–846), known for his accessible style and social critique, alongside his contemporary Yüan Chen.

memes.label: Ch'in Dynasty
memes.description: The first imperial dynasty of a unified China (221–206 B.C.), founded by Shih Huang Ti, which standardized writing, weights, measures, and established the administrative template for all subsequent Chinese empires.

memes.label: Red Eyebrows Rebellion
memes.description: The Red Eyebrows Rebellion (c. 18–24 CE) was a massive peasant uprising against the usurper Wang Mang's Hsin dynasty, named for the rebels' practice of painting their eyebrows red as a badge of membership, which contributed directly to the restoration of the Han dynasty under Kuang-wu Ti.

memes.label: Republican China
memes.description: The republican form of government established in China after the 1911 Revolution that overthrew the Ch'ing dynasty, encompassing the warlord period (1912–1927), Nationalist rule under the Kuomintang (1927–1949), and the regime's retreat to Taiwan.

memes.label: Shang Dynasty
memes.description: The earliest historically attested Chinese dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BC), centered in the Yellow River valley, known for oracle bone inscriptions, advanced bronze metallurgy, and a theocratic state organized around the high god Shang Ti.

memes.label: Shang Yang
memes.description: The most famous representative of the Legalist school and the actual organizer of the state of Ch'in (died 338 BC).

memes.label: Shao Yung
memes.description: A Sung dynasty philosopher-poet (1011–1077) and leader of the Loyang School, known for his contributions to Neo-Confucian thought and Sung literature.

memes.label: Shen Tsung
memes.description: Temple name shared by two Chinese emperors — Song Shen Tsung (r. 1067–1085), who sponsored Wang An-shih's reforms, and Ming Shen Tsung (Wan-li Emperor, r. 1572–1620), under whom dynastic decline accelerated.

memes.label: Shih Hu
memes.description: Emperor of the Later Chao dynasty (r. 334–349), known for his military campaigns and his reign's impact on northern China during the Six Dynasties period.

memes.label: Shih Huang-ti
memes.description: First Emperor of China (r. 221–210 BCE) who unified the warring states, standardized writing and measures, and founded the Ch'in dynasty — China's first centralized imperial state.

memes.label: Shih Lo
memes.description: Former Hun slave who rose to become a military leader and founder of the Later Chao dynasty (329–352), ruling a wide empire in North China through nomadic warrior methods rather than Chinese administrative models.
aliases: Shih Lo of the Later Chao

memes.label: Sino-Japanese War (1894–1895)
memes.description: The 1894–1895 war between Qing China and Meiji Japan over control of Korea, ending in Chinese defeat and the Treaty of Shimonoseki.

memes.label: Su Tsung
memes.description: T'ang dynasty emperor (r. 756-762) who ascended the throne during the An Lu-shan Rebellion and led the imperial resistance from north-western Shensi.

memes.label: Su Tung-p'o
memes.description: The greatest poet of the Sung dynasty (1036–1101), renowned for introducing everyday vernacular expressions into classical Chinese poetry with unprecedented emotional genuineness.

memes.label: Sui Dynasty
memes.description: The short-lived dynasty (A.D. 580–618) that reunified China after nearly four centuries of division between north and south, known for the construction of the Grand Canal and the establishment of administrative foundations later perfected by the T'ang.

memes.label: Sun Yat-sen
memes.description: Chinese revolutionary leader (1866–1925) who led the overthrow of the Ch'ing dynasty, served as the first provisional president of the Republic of China, and founded the Kuomintang (Nationalist Party).

memes.label: Sung Dynasty
memes.description: Imperial dynasty of China (960–1279) founded by Chao K'uang-yin, divided into the Northern Sung (960–1127) and Southern Sung (1127–1279) periods, known for economic revolution, cultural efflorescence, and eventual Mongol conquest.

memes.label: T'ai P'ing Rebellion
memes.description: A massive peasant rebellion (1851–1864) led by Hung Hsiu-ch'üan that established the T'ai P'ing Heavenly Kingdom in Nanking and nearly toppled the Manchu Qing dynasty.

memes.label: T'ai Tsung
memes.description: Second emperor of the T'ang dynasty (r. 627–649), whose reign marked the zenith of T'ang power and Chinese prestige in Central Asia.

memes.label: T'ang Dynasty
memes.description: The T'ang dynasty (A.D. 618–906) was an imperial dynasty of China that presided over one of the greatest periods of Chinese power, culture, and territorial expansion, widely regarded as a golden age of Chinese civilization.

memes.label: Taoism
memes.description: Taoism is an indigenous Chinese philosophical and religious tradition founded on the teachings of Lao Tzu and Chuang Tzu, emphasizing harmony with the Tao (the Way of the universe), the principle of wu-wei (non-action), and withdrawal from social life into nature.

memes.label: Three Kingdoms Period
memes.description: The period of the first division of China (AD 220–280) into three rival kingdoms — Wei, Wu, and Shu Han — following the fall of the Han dynasty.

memes.label: Tou Ku
memes.description: Tou Ku (Dou Gu), Eastern Han dynasty general and leader of the Tou clan who led campaigns against the Northern Hsiung-nu and sponsored Pan Ch'ao's expedition to the Western Regions (1st century CE).

memes.label: Ts'ao Ts'ao
memes.description: A prominent warlord and general during the late Han dynasty and early Three Kingdoms period, who controlled northern China and laid the foundation for the Wei kingdom.

memes.label: Tsêng Kuo-fan
memes.description: A prominent Qing dynasty Confucian scholar-general who organized provincial armies to suppress the Taiping Rebellion and initiated the Self-Strengthening Movement.

memes.label: Tsewang Rabdan
memes.description: A khan of the Ölöt (Dzungar) Mongols who rose against Qing China in 1715, triggering wars that led to Chinese conquest of Mongolia and eastern Turkestan.

memes.label: Tso Tsung-t'ang
memes.description: A famous Chinese general of the late Qing dynasty (1812–1885) who suppressed rebellions and reconquered Turkestan from Yakub Beg.

memes.label: Tsou Yen
memes.description: Philosopher of the Warring States period (fl. 320–260 B.C.) who founded the "Yin-Yang" and "Five Elements" (Wu-hsing) cosmological school, combining natural science with political theory in a system that profoundly influenced later Chinese thought.

memes.label: Tu Fu
memes.description: One of the two greatest poets of the Tang dynasty (712–770), renowned alongside Li Po for bringing genuine feeling and perfection of form to classical Chinese poetry.

memes.label: Tung Chung-shu
memes.description: Han dynasty Confucian philosopher (179–104 BC) who established Confucianism as the official state ideology and developed the system by which the Confucian classics — especially the Ch'un-ch'iu — were treated as a body of legal precedents binding on the educated gentry class.

memes.label: Uighurs
memes.description: The Uighurs were a Turkish people of Central Asia who served as critical military allies of the T'ang dynasty during the An Lu-shan rebellion, adopted Manichaeism as their state religion, and later became influential administrators and merchants under the Mongol Empire.

memes.label: Wang An-shih
memes.description: Chinese statesman and reformer (1021–1086) of the Sung dynasty who led a comprehensive reform program aimed at restructuring state finances, agriculture, and the examination system.

memes.label: Wang Chen
memes.description: A powerful eunuch of the Ming dynasty who dominated the court of the young Ying Tsung emperor and whose disastrous military campaign against the Oirat Mongols in 1449 led to the emperor's capture at the Tumu Crisis.

memes.label: Wang Ch'ung
memes.description: A Han dynasty philosopher (27–c. 97 CE) known for his rationalist skepticism and empirical approach to knowledge, author of the Lun Heng (Discourses Weighed in the Balance).

memes.label: Wang Mang
memes.description: Wang Mang (c. 45 BCE – 23 CE) was a Han dynasty official who usurped the throne and founded the short-lived Hsin dynasty (9–23 CE), known for his ambitious but ultimately failed program of radical social and economic reforms.

memes.label: Wang Tun
memes.description: Powerful military governor and rebel during the Eastern Tsin dynasty (early 4th century CE) whose uprising from the Hankow region threatened the imperial capital at Nanking and set a pattern for regional military challenges to weak central authority.

memes.label: Wang Yang-ming
memes.description: Ming dynasty philosopher-general who developed the School of Intuition, challenging Neo-Confucian orthodoxy and influencing political thought in both China and Japan.

memes.label: Wei Dynasty
memes.description: One of the Three Kingdoms of China (A.D. 220–265), founded by Ts'ao P'ei in the north after the fall of the Han dynasty, which controlled the most populous regions of the former empire but failed to reunify the realm.

memes.label: Wen Ti (Emperor Wen of Han)
memes.description: Emperor Wen of Han (r. 180–157 BC), whose frugal and peaceful reign brought economic recovery, population growth, and the first penetration of Confucian ideals into the imperial administration.

memes.label: White Lotus Rebellion
memes.description: The White Lotus Rebellion (1774–1802) was a major popular uprising organised by the secret White Lotus Society (Pai-lien-hui) against the Ch'ing dynasty, centred in Honan, Anhui, and the western provinces, suppressed only after a massive military campaign and enormous government expenditure.

memes.label: K. A. Wittfogel
memes.description: German-American sinologist and social theorist (1896–1988), author of Oriental Despotism (1957) and co-author of the standard history of the Liao dynasty, whose theory of hydraulic civilization significantly influenced the study of Chinese political economy.

memes.label: Wu San-kui
memes.description: Wu San-kui (1612–1678), the Ming general whose alliance with the Manchus enabled their conquest of China, and who later declared himself emperor in a failed rebellion against the Qing dynasty.

memes.label: Wu Ti (Emperor Wu of Han)
memes.description: Emperor Wu of Han (r. 141–86 BC), whose active foreign policy and military campaigns expanded Chinese dominion into Central Asia, Korea, and southern China, laying the foundations of the imperial state.

memes.label: Wu Wang
memes.description: Founder and first ruler of the Chou dynasty (r. c. 1028–? BCE) who overthrew the Shang, established the longest-ruling dynasty in Chinese history, and inaugurated the feudal system that defined early Chinese state organization.

memes.label: Yakub Beg
memes.description: Ruler of Kashgar and Turkestan (r. 1866–1877) who led a major rebellion against Ch'ing authority in Central Asia, established an independent state with Ottoman and Russian recognition, and whose death paved the way for Chinese reconquest.

memes.label: Yang Ti
memes.description: The second emperor of the Sui dynasty, known for ambitious canal construction, extravagant rule, military campaigns against Korea, and the eventual collapse of his reign.

memes.label: Yangtze River
memes.description: The Yangtze River is the longest river in China and the historic economic, demographic, and cultural heartland of the south, whose fertile valley has sustained dynasties, commerce, and civilization for millennia and whose lower reaches became the centre of Chinese industry and modernization from the Sung period onward.

memes.label: Yellow Turban Rebellion
memes.description: The Yellow Turban Rebellion (184 CE) was a large-scale popular uprising against the Eastern Han dynasty, led by the Taoist secret society of Chang Ling, which fatally weakened the central government and inaugurated a century of warlordism that ended the Han dynasty.

memes.label: Yen Fu
memes.description: A pioneering Chinese translator of the late Qing period who introduced Western philosophical, scientific, and social thought to China through his translations.

memes.label: Yo Fei
memes.description: Yo Fei (1103–1142), celebrated Song dynasty general who led successful campaigns against the Jurchen Jin dynasty before being executed on trumped-up charges following a peace treaty.

memes.label: Yü Liang
memes.description: A powerful general and regent of the Eastern Chin dynasty (4th century CE) who dominated the court through his clique and crushed the Su Chün rebellion.

memes.label: Yüan Dynasty
memes.description: The Mongol dynasty of China (A.D. 1280–1368), founded by Khubilai Khan, which incorporated China into the vast Mongol world empire and introduced a period of foreign rule, cultural exchange, and economic transformation.

memes.label: Yüan Shih-k'ai
memes.description: Late Ch'ing military commander who became the first president of the Republic of China (1912–1916) and briefly attempted to restore the monarchy with himself as emperor.

memes.label: Yüan Ti
memes.description: Name used by two significant Chinese emperors — the Han ruler whose widow enabled Wang Mang's rise, and the founder of the Eastern Chin dynasty (r. 317–323) who re-established Chinese rule south of the Yangtze.

memes.label: Yung-lo Emperor
memes.description: Third emperor of the Ming dynasty (r. 1403–1424), who moved the capital to Peking, commissioned the Cheng Ho naval expeditions, and presided over the most magnificent period of the Ming epoch.
